In addition, the councilmember must disclose her
the Stockton partnership because the stockton
is doing business in her jurisdictj,on.
FACTS
Councilmember Lewis has an investment interest in a
partnership which is active in real estate transactions in the
city of Stockton. The partnership consi.sts of two general
partners and 15 lirnited partners, of which councilmember Lewis is
one of the tinited partners. Each limited partner has invested
more than $2,000 in the partnership. You stated that
Councilmember Le\"ris' interest constitutes less than 102 of the
partnership .
The two general, partners are also actj.ve in the San Jose
area. You stated that the general partners rnaintain a different
partnership in San Jose. The only connection to the Stockton
partnership is the fact that the same general Partners control
both partnerships and the adninistrative aspects of the Stockton
partnership are handled at the offices of the san Jose
partnership .
ANALYS I S
The Political Reform Act (the ttAct") , was enacted by the
people of the state of California by initiative 
in 1974. The
purpose for the conf I ict-of- interest provisions of the Act was
to ensure that public officials, 
whether elected or appointed,
would perforrn thej,r duties in an iropartial, nanner, free frorn bias
caused by their own financial interests or the financial interests
of persons $rho have supported thern. (Section 81001.(b).)
In furtherance of this goaI, Section 87100 of the Act
prohibits any public official 
froro naking, participating in rnak-
ing, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence
a governmentaL decision in which the official 
has a financial
j-nterest. A "public official" 
is defined in section 82048 and
Regul-ation 18700 (copy enclosed) as every natural- person who is a
member, officer, employee, or consultant of a state or 1ocal
government agency, As a San Jose city councilmember,
counciLnenber Lewis is a public officia] 
as defined in the Act.
Economic Interests
Section 87103 speei.f ies that an official 
has a financial
interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material- financial effect, dj.stinguishable
fron the effect on the public generally, on the official 
or a
member of his or her immediate fanily or on:


Fi■e No.
Page 3
I-90-325
(a) Any business entity in which the public
official 
has a di.rect or indirect investment e/orth
one thousand dollars ($1,0oo) or more.
(c) Any source of income, other than gifts and
other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public liithout regard to
official 
status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($25o) or more in value provided to,
recej.ved by or pronised to the public official
within 1.2 months prior to the tine when the
decision is nade.
(d) Any business entity in which the public
officiaf 
is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or hol,ds any position of management.
Section 87103 (a) , (c) and (d) .
Thus, Councilmember Lewis nay not make, participate in rnaking
or in any way attempt to use her official 
position to influence a
governmental decision in which she knoh/s or has reason to know she
has an financial- interest. 
(Section 87100.)
In your letter you described the councilmember's sole
economic interest in question as an investment in the Stockton
partnership. You stated that councilmember Lewis has an
investment in the stockton partnership of more than $l-,000, and is
a rinited Partner in the paitnership.3/ 
Ho$'ever, section 82034
defines rri,nvestmenttr for purPoses of the Act to include only
financial interests if the business entity or any parent,
subsidiary or other!,rise related business entitv has an interest in
real property in the jurisdiction, 
or does business or plans to do
business in the jurisdiction, 
or has done business within the
jurisdiction at any tirne during the two years prior to the tine
iny statement or other action is requirel undei this title.{/
Regul,ation 18236 (copy enclosed) defines rrotherwise related
business entitytt as folIohIS:
Jt Presumably she has also received income from the partnershj.p.
If the incorne was more than 9250 in the l-ast 12 months, then the
j-ncome is an additj-onaI grounds for considering the partnership
itself as an econornic interest of the councilmember.
You stated in your facts that Counci■
member Lewis
1oZ or more of the partnership. Please note that if
councilmember's j-nterest does becone 10? or more, a
of investrnents of the partnership will be treated as
interests of the counciLmember. (Section 82034.)
4/
does not own
the
pro-rata share
investment'


Fi■e No.  I-90-325
Page 4
(b) Business entities, including corpora-
tions, partnerships, joint ventures and any other
organizations and enterprises operated for profit,
which do not have a parent-subsidiary relationship
are othererise related if any one of the f olJ.owing
three tests is met:
(1) one business entity has a control-
ling ownership interest in the other business
entity.
(2) There is shared management and
control- bet$reen the entities. 
In deterrnining
whether there is shared management and
control, consideration shoul,d be given to the
following factors:
(A) The same person or
substantiallv the same Derson owns and
manages the two entities,
(B) There are conmon or comrnj-ngled
funds or assets;
(c) The business entities share the
use of the same offices or emplovees, or
otherwise share activities, 
resources or
personnel on a requl-ar basis;
(D) There is otherwise a regular
and close working relationship betl^reen
the entities; or
(3) A controlling owner (50? or greater
interest as a shareholder or as a general
partner) 5/ in one entity also is a controfling
owner in the othbr entity.
Regulation La236, footnote and
emphasis added.
Thus, if either the Stockton partnership or the San Jose
partnership which is otherwise related to the Stockton partnership
does business in San Jose, the councilmember's interest in the
Stockton partnership would be disclosable. You stated that the
adrninistrative activities of the Stockton partnership are handled
at the San Jose offices of the San Jose partnership. We believe
that this link alone wou.ld be sufficient to rneet the "doing
businesstr requirement of the statute. 
While such activity night
5/ rn 1985,
contro 1l ing
than 50?.
the Cornmission adopted
ownership interest as ana policy interpreting a
ownership j-nterest of nore


Fi■e No.
Page 5
I-90-325
not constitute 'tdoing businesstr in the jurisdiction under other
provisions of 1aw, we believe that these other sections are not
deterrninative because the other standards were enacted for
purposes different than the purposes of personal financial
disclosure under the Act. (In re Batv (1979) 5 FPPC Ops. 10, copy
enclosed. )
An additional reason for concluding that the stockton
partnership is an investment interest of the councilmember is
because the San Jose partnership is otherwise related to the
Stockton partnership due to the same general partners. According
to your facts the san Jose partnership has interests i,n real
property and does business in the councilmember's iurisdiction.
-oniequ-ntly, the stockton Partnership is an investment interest
of the councj-lmenber. (Section a2o34.)
Further, Regulation 18706 (copy enclosed) provides that for
conflict of interest purposes an official 
has a financial interest
in a decision within the meanj-ng of Section 871oo if it is
reasonably foreseeabfe that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable fron its effect on the public
generally′  On a business entity which is otherwise related to a
(Requlation 1a236. ) Thus, councilmember Lewis has an
interest in the partnership in San Jose because that
has the same general Partners.
However, the analysis does not end with the respective
business entities. 
In 1983 in In re Nord (8 FPPC ops. 6, copy
enclosed) the Comnission addressed a similar question concerning
the Merced city Attorney and his interest in a limited
partnership. In that opj.nion the Cornmission considered the roles
of tfre partners in a lirnited partnership. The Commission stated:
ItThe general partners have fuIl control over the management of the
firm [and are] personally responsible for the debts and
out-igitions oi tne firm, as in the case of ordinary partnerships,
without regard to the anounts contributed by his or her to the
firrn capital...The liability 
of the generaf partner for the debts
of the iirn has been heLd...to extend not onl-y to his or her
interest in the partnership but to al,I his or her private
DroDertv.rr (Nord, supra, D. 8.)
conversely, "the limited partners are not bound by the
obligations of the partnership, thus their liability 
is
'Iimited. "' 
(Noril, supra, p. 8.) Thus, the limited partnershj-p
franework "involves an investment by the lirnited partners in the
entrepreneurial skiLls of the general partner, who is charged with
the sole discretion and .authority to manage the investment by
conducting the partnership business.rr (Nord, suDra, P. 9. ) "In
doing so, the lirnited partner is really investing in the generaL
partnerrf personally. 
(Nord, supra, p. L2.)
The commission concluded in light of the Act's clear
intention to prohibit conflicts of interest arising from financia]
economrc
partnership


Fi■e No.
Page 6
■-90-325
relationships, that an investment by a Linited partner in the
partnership aLso is an investment in the controll'ing general
lartners. 
Thus CounciLmember Lewis also has an econonic interest
in the general partners of the partnership individually.
In summation, counciLmember Lelris has an economic interest j'n
the stockton partnership, the general partners of the stockton
partnership personally and in any business entity that is
otherwise related to the stockton partnership, including the San
Jose partnership.
Disoual-ification
For an economic interest to be disqualifying with respect to
a specific governmental decision, it must be reasonably
forlseeable that the decisi.on will have a material financial
effect, distingiuishable from its effect on the public generally,
on the economic interest. 
consequentty, councilmember Lewis nay
not participate in any decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a rnaterial financial effect on any
econornic interest.
whether the financial consequences of a deci-sion are reason-
ably foreseeable at the tirne a governmental decision is made
depinds on the facts of each particular case. An effect is
coirsidered reasonably foreseelbLe if there is a substantial
likelihood that it wil.1 occur. certainty is not required.
Houever, if an effect is only a mere possibility, 
it is not
reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198,
copy enclosed. )
In addition, the foreseeable financial effect on the
councilmember's economic interest must also be material to require
disqualification. 
The test for materiality differs depending on
the specific circumstances of each decision. For exanple, wirere
an economic interest is directty before the city council, the
effect is qenerallv deemed to be naterial and disqualification is
required.6/ (Regulation ta702.1(a) , copy enclosed. )
where an economic interest is not directly before the city
council, but rnay be indirectly affected, Regulations 18702.2
through aa702.6 app1y. - negulitions 18702.2 and L8702-6 (copies
encloied) deal specifically with sources of income and
5/ An econornic interest is directly before the city council- when
the economic interest initiates the proceeding by filing 
an
application, claim, appeial, or sj,milar request, or is a named
plity in, or the subject of, the proceeding. A person or business
Lntity is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the
issuance, rene$/al, approval, denial or revocation of any license,
perrnit, or other entitlernent to, or contract with, the subject
person or business entity. (Regulation t8702.1(b).)


Fi■e No.  I-90-325
Page 7
j-nvestments. we have enclosed a confl ict-of-interest 
pamphfet
which discusses these standards, for your information.
Di-sc Losure
Under the Act, every public official, rnust disclose all his or
her investments, real property interests, sources of incorne and
sources of qifts that coul-d foreseeably be affected by the
exercise of the official's 
duties. 
(Sections 81002(c), a72OO-
87313.) 
Consequently, the Stockton partnership should be
discl-osed on Councilmember Lewis' statement of econornic interest
because the partnership is a business entity in which the
councj.lmember has an investment interest of greater than $L,000
and is doing business in her jurisdiction. 
The councilmember need
not disclosi the separate san Jose partnership nor the individual
partners despite the fact that they are potentially disqualifying
economi-c interests.
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (915) 322-590]-.
Sincerely,

ohn W。

FACTS Councilmember Lewis has an investment interest in a partnership which is active in real estate transactions in the city of Stockton. The partnership consi.sts of two general partners and 15 lirnited partners, of which councilmember Lewis is one of the tinited partners. Each limited partner has invested more than $2,000 in the partnership. You stated that Councilmember Le\"ris' interest constitutes less than 102 of the partnership . The two general, partners are also actj.ve in the San Jose area. You stated that the general partners rnaintain a different partnership in San Jose.
